column1: What problem does the Strategy Pattern solve?
column2: It lets you swap algorithms/behaviors without changing the caller code.

column1: When should I use Strategy Pattern?
column2: Use it when one behavior has multiple variants and new variants are expected over time.

column1: When should I NOT use Strategy Pattern?
column2: Do not use it when there is only one stable behavior and no expected variation.

column1: What is the core idea of Strategy Pattern?
column2: Encapsulate each algorithm in its own class behind a common interface.

column1: What are the main participants in Strategy Pattern?
column2: Context, Strategy interface, and Concrete Strategy implementations.

column1: What does the Context do in Strategy Pattern?
column2: It delegates work to the currently selected strategy.

column1: How is Strategy different from a big if-else/switch block?
column2: Strategy removes branching from one large method and localizes each behavior in its own class.

column1: What principle does Strategy support strongly?
column2: Open/Closed Principle: add new strategy classes instead of modifying existing context logic.

column1: How does Strategy reduce blast radius?
column2: Changing one algorithm affects only one strategy class instead of a central branching method.

column1: How does Strategy improve testing?
column2: Each strategy can be unit-tested in isolation; context tests focus on wiring/delegation.

column1: What is a common trade-off of Strategy Pattern?
column2: More classes/indirection, which can feel heavy for simple cases.

column1: How do you choose a strategy at runtime?
column2: Inject it (constructor/setter/factory) based on config, request type, or user input.

column1: Payment example of Strategy Pattern?
column2: PaymentContext uses PaymentStrategy interface; CardStrategy/PayPalStrategy/WalletStrategy are interchangeable.

column1: CSV example of Strategy Pattern?
column2: CsvExporter depends on Sanitizer interface; StrictSanitizer and PermissiveSanitizer are swappable strategies.

column1: How is Strategy different from Template Method?
column2: Template Method uses inheritance and fixed skeleton; Strategy uses composition and runtime-swappable behavior.

column1: How is Strategy different from State Pattern?
column2: Strategy chooses algorithm externally; State changes behavior based on internal object state transitions.

column1: What is a good naming convention for strategy interfaces?
column2: Use behavior-focused names like PaymentStrategy, PricingStrategy, Sanitizer.

column1: What is a red flag that suggests Strategy refactor?
column2: A method keeps growing with conditionals for variants and breaks often when adding a new case.

column1: Interview one-liner for Strategy Pattern?
column2: Strategy Pattern defines a family of algorithms, encapsulates each, and makes them interchangeable via composition.

column1: How to explain Strategy in 30 seconds?
column2: When behavior has multiple evolving variants, put each variant behind a common interface and let a context delegate to the chosen implementation.

column1: What is behavior in OOD?
column2: Behavior is what an object does: the actions and decisions it can perform through its methods.

column1: How is behavior different from state in OOD?
column2: State is what an object knows (data/fields); behavior is what an object does (methods/logic).

column1: Why is behavior important in OOD?
column2: Good object design centers on behavior so responsibilities are clear and changes stay localized.

column1: What does "encapsulate behavior" mean?
column2: Put related logic behind a clear interface so callers use it without depending on internal details.

column1: How do you spot misplaced behavior?
column2: If a class mostly reads/writes another class's data to do work, behavior likely belongs in the other class.

column1: What is an example of behavior in a payment domain?
column2: Methods like authorize(), capture(), and refund() are behaviors because they execute business actions.

column1: What is "behavioral cohesion"?
column2: Methods in a class should serve one responsibility and change for similar reasons.

column1: How does poor behavior design increase blast radius?
column2: When unrelated behaviors are mixed in one class, small changes can break many paths and tests.

column1: In OOD, what does "tell, don't ask" mean?
column2: Tell objects to do work instead of pulling their data out and making decisions elsewhere.

column1: How is Strategy related to behavior in OOD?
column2: Strategy extracts variable behavior into interchangeable objects behind one interface.

column1: Show a minimal Strategy Pattern code example in TypeScript.
column2: interface PaymentStrategy { pay(amount: number): string; } class CardPayment implements PaymentStrategy { pay(amount: number): string { return `Paid $${amount} by card`; } } class PaypalPayment implements PaymentStrategy { pay(amount: number): string { return `Paid $${amount} by PayPal`; } } class PaymentContext { constructor(private strategy: PaymentStrategy) {} setStrategy(strategy: PaymentStrategy): void { this.strategy = strategy; } checkout(amount: number): string { return this.strategy.pay(amount); } } const ctx = new PaymentContext(new CardPayment()); ctx.checkout(50); ctx.setStrategy(new PaypalPayment()); ctx.checkout(50);

column1: What is a concrete real-world example of Strategy Pattern?
column2: Payment processing: `PaymentContext` depends on `PaymentStrategy`, while `CardPayment`, `PaypalPayment`, and `WalletPayment` provide different payment algorithms that can be swapped at runtime.

column1: How does the caller switch strategies in code?
column2: The caller injects a different implementation into context, e.g. `context.setStrategy(new PaypalPayment())`, then calls the same `checkout()` method.

column1: What unit tests would you write for a Strategy Pattern payment example?
column2: Test each strategy class behavior independently (Card/PayPal/Wallet), then test `PaymentContext` delegation and strategy switching without retesting payment internals.

column1: Show a concrete CSV sanitization Strategy example.
column2: interface Sanitizer { sanitize(value: string): string; } class StrictSanitizer implements Sanitizer { sanitize(v: string): string { return v.replace(/[^\w\s-]/g, ''); } } class PermissiveSanitizer implements Sanitizer { sanitize(v: string): string { return v.trim(); } } class CsvExporter { constructor(private sanitizer: Sanitizer) {} setSanitizer(s: Sanitizer): void { this.sanitizer = s; } exportCell(raw: string): string { return this.sanitizer.sanitize(raw); } }

column1: What is behavior localization in OOD?
column2: Behavior localization means keeping a behavior in one focused place, close to the data and rules it belongs to, instead of spreading it across many classes.

column1: Why is behavior localization important?
column2: It reduces blast radius, improves readability, and makes testing/debugging easier because behavior changes happen in one place.

column1: Give a delocalized behavior example.
column2: Bad example: discount logic is partly in controller, partly in service, and partly in utility methods, so changing one discount rule requires edits in multiple files.

column1: Give a localized behavior example.
column2: Good example: all discount rules live in `DiscountPolicy` (or `PricingStrategy`) and callers delegate to it, so a rule change happens in one class.

column1: How can I detect poor behavior localization?
column2: If one behavior change requires touching 3+ places, behavior is likely delocalized.

column1: How is behavior localization related to cohesion?
column2: Localized behavior increases cohesion because a class has one clear responsibility and related methods change for the same reason.

column1: How is behavior localization related to "Tell, Don't Ask"?
column2: With localization, callers tell an object to do work instead of pulling data out and doing the logic elsewhere.

column1: What is a concrete payment localization example?
column2: `PaymentContext` delegates payment processing to `PaymentStrategy`; payment algorithms are localized in `CardPayment`, `PaypalPayment`, etc., not mixed in one giant method.

column1: What is a concrete CSV localization example?
column2: `CsvExporter` delegates cell cleaning to `Sanitizer`; sanitization rules are localized in sanitizer classes instead of scattered across exporter, controller, and utility files.

column1: What is a common trade-off of behavior localization?
column2: You may introduce more classes/interfaces, but gain maintainability and safer future changes.

column1: What is indirection in OOD?
column2: Indirection means accessing behavior through an intermediate abstraction (like an interface or adapter) instead of calling a concrete implementation directly.

column1: Give a simple direct vs indirect example.
column2: Direct: `exporter.sanitize(value)` calls concrete code in the exporter. Indirect: `exporter` calls `sanitizer.sanitize(value)` through a `Sanitizer` interface.

column1: What is a good indirection example in payments?
column2: `CheckoutService` depends on `PaymentGateway` interface; concrete implementations like `StripeGateway` or `PaypalGateway` can be swapped without changing checkout logic.

column1: What is a good indirection example in notifications?
column2: `Notifier` depends on `NotificationChannel` interface; `EmailChannel`, `SmsChannel`, and `PushChannel` are interchangeable implementations.

column1: What is a good indirection example in storage?
column2: `UserRepository` depends on `UserStore` abstraction; you can switch from `PostgresUserStore` to `DynamoUserStore` without changing domain logic.

column1: How does indirection reduce coupling?
column2: Callers depend on abstractions, so implementation changes stay behind the interface and do not force caller edits.

column1: How does indirection help testing?
column2: You can inject a fake/mock implementation through the same interface to isolate and test caller behavior.

column1: What is over-indirection?
column2: Adding abstractions with no real variation or change pressure, which makes code harder to follow without clear benefit.

column1: Give an over-indirection example.
column2: Creating `ISanitizeService`, `SanitizeManager`, and `SanitizeProvider` when there is one stable sanitization rule and no expected variants.

column1: When is indirection worth the cost?
column2: When behavior is likely to vary, external dependencies may change, or you need clear seams for testing and modularity.

column1: What is the trade-off of indirection?
column2: You gain flexibility and safer change boundaries, but pay with extra classes, wiring, and mental hops during debugging.

column1: Interview one-liner: indirection in OOD?
column2: Indirection introduces an abstraction layer between caller and implementation to reduce coupling and improve substitutability, at the cost of added complexity.
